parentQuestion: A patient with severe burns shows minimal scarring after healing, with near-complete regeneration of the epidermis. However, deep dermal burns in the same patient resulted in thick, fibrotic scars. What determines whether repair results in regeneration versus fibrosis?

scaffolds.q: REGENERATION means restoring the original tissue type.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The damaged tissue is replaced with identical, functional tissue. Best outcome!

scaffolds.q: FIBROSIS means replacing damaged tissue with scar tissue.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Scar tissue is collagen-rich but doesn't function like the original tissue.

scaffolds.q: Some tissues regenerate well (skin, bone, liver), others don't (heart, brain).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Tissue TYPE matters. Epithelia have stem cells and regenerate well. Cardiac muscle doesn't.

scaffolds.q: The SEVERITY of damage affects whether regeneration or fibrosis occurs.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Mild damage may regenerate. Severe damage often leads to scarring.

scaffolds.q: Superficial burns leave the basement membrane intact, allowing regeneration.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The basement membrane is the scaffold for epithelial cells to migrate and regenerate.

scaffolds.q: Deep burns destroy the basement membrane, leading to fibrosis/scarring.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! No scaffold = no template for regeneration. The body defaults to scar tissue.

scaffolds.q: BOTH tissue type AND severity of damage determine regeneration vs. fibrosis.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Regenerative capacity + injury depth together determine the outcome.

scaffolds.q: Same patient, different depths of burn = different outcomes (regeneration vs. scarring).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Superficial burn regenerated (basement membrane intact). Deep burn scarred (basement membrane destroyed).

scaffolds.q: The answer is: tissue TYPE and SEVERITY of damage determine outcome.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not just age, not just granulation presence, not just inflammation. Both tissue type AND damage severity matter.
